data.title: FactSet: 3 Appealing Characteristics That Make It A Buy
data.description:
Throughout my writing on Seeking Alpha, I have been pretty bullish on companies in the financial data industry, such as MSCI (MSCI), S&P Global (SPGI), and Moody's (MCO). However, with a market cap of $17.5 billion, which is much smaller than the companies above, FactSet Research Systems (NYSE:FDS) is another company that I am bullish on, and in this analysis, I will explain my reasons for providing a "buy" rating on the stock.

Company Description

FactSet is a data solutions enterprise that offers a wide range of solutions for portfolio management, risk, and reporting workflows to institutional clients such as asset managers, hedge funds, endowments, investment banks, and insurance companies.

They offer subscription-based services, and their core product is the FactSet Workstation, which is comparable to Capital IQ, Refinitiv Eikon, and the Bloomberg Terminal. Although other competitors have a larger market share, FactSet differentiates itself by offering a lower subscription price of $12,000 per year, which makes it attractive to investment banking boutiques and small family offices, serving 219,141 users.

Moreover, they have more stripped-down versions of the FactSet Workstation, including the recently introduced Pitch Creator. This is a platform that uses AI to automate investment banking presentations, allowing junior bankers to save time on tedious tasks such as reformatting presentations and updating charts. Simultaneously, they have other solutions for business development integrations with Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics.

Three Appealing Characteristics of FactSet

  1. The core KPI that makes me bullish on the company's long-term value is its high client retention rate, currently at 91% and consistently above 90% over the past eighteen quarters. Although many competitors are offering similar solutions, teams face high switching costs when changing from one platform to another. Additionally, many Excel spreadsheets are already integrated with FactSet data, making it time-consuming to change the data provider. At the same time, the retention rate of the annual subscription value (ASV) has been higher than 95% for the past years.
  2. FactSet, by its nature, is a capital-light company, which enables it to achieve high margins and strong performance metrics. Over the past twelve months, they have achieved an enviable return on common equity of 28.12% and a return on assets of 12.79%, complemented by a gross profit margin of 54.15% and an EBIT margin of 31.58%. At the same time, they have a robust interest coverage ratio of 11.72x.
  3. Although ASV growth rates have been decreasing over the past two years, declining from 9.1% in Q2 2023 to 4.1% in Q2 2025, there is a long-term growth opportunity to innovate with Gen AI solutions and upsell them to existing clients. During the earnings call, management indicated that their teams are well-equipped to deliver H2 growth based on their broad range of solutions and accelerating client interest in Gen AI solutions.

FDS: Shares are Fairly Valued

Back in April, with the market turmoil, the forward PE ratio valuation of FactSet was at historically cheap levels of 21.76x, but with the market recovery, the stock of FDS did the same and now has a valuation of 25.26x. Although the price became considerably more expensive than a month ago, the current valuation is similar to the historical average of 26.11x, with a slight margin of safety of 3.26%.

Unfortunately, Bloomberg is private, and competitors such as Capital IQ and Refinitiv Eikon are owned by S&P Global and the London Stock Exchange (OTCPK:LDNXF). A multiple comparison with them would be too distorted, as the terminals aren't the core business of these companies. Nonetheless, Morningstar (MORN) and its platform Morningstar Direct could work as a close peer, and currently they are trading at a higher forward PE of 33.57x compared to FDS.

Risks

Although there was a revenue guidance boost in the previous earnings call, management decreased guidance on GAAP metrics, bringing the expected FY 2025 diluted EPS to a mid-point of $15.1 from the previous $15.4. However, CFO Helen Shan commented that this decrease is due to one-time non-recurring expenses attributed to acquisitions of Irwin, LiquidityBook, and LogoIntern.

At the same time, the company has been exhibiting a downward trend in its cash position that has decreased considerably from above $800 million to $287 million in three years. In addition, their current ratio of 1.47x is at historical lows. Yet, this is counterbalanced by a consistent positive operating cash flow generation over the past years, attributing the cash decreases to cash acquisitions and debt repayments.

Takeaway

Financial data companies such as FactSet are among my favorites. They exhibit a high client retention rate, which makes the LTV of a client considerably high, bringing more predictable cash flows that allow management to be better informed in making corporate decisions. Although the competition offers similar products, they have a price advantage that makes their offering appealing, especially to the sell-side that generally doesn't require an expensive Bloomberg Terminal to get their data from.

Their innovation of Gen AI solutions would help teams' productivity, and therefore, clients would be inclined to increase their expenses with FactSet products, making this a long-term investment opportunity.

Currently, their valuation is fairly valued compared to their historical performance, and the shares yield a dividend of approximately 1%, which was recently increased by 6% to $1.10. In my opinion, although the multiple has considerably expanded since April, this is a solid company with great products for institutional clients, and therefore, I will rate the shares of FactSet as a "buy."

data.title: UAE: This Emirati ETF Faces Significant Concentration Risk
data.description:
Investment Thesis

iShares MSCI UAE ETF (NASDAQ:UAE) allows foreign investors to easily get some exposure to the United Arab Emirates, a seemingly sensible idea on the basis of the country's exponential growth over the last few decades.

My argument, however, is that the combination of equities held by the fund represent a poor proxy for the success of the Emirati economy. The ETF also comes with unique risks and a history of poor performance versus both the U.S. stock market and other single country ETFs, as well as a relatively high expense ratio. Taken together, these considerations lead to me assigning a sell rating to the fund.

The Emirati Economy

Given that it is among the fastest growing economies of the past half century, it is quite easy to understand why some might seek exposure to the UAE.

Led by the two largest emirates, Abu Dhabi and Dubai, the UAE has increased its GDP (in dollar terms) by 393% between 2000 and 2023, representing a 7.2% CAGR. Over an even longer timeframe, starting in 1970, the CAGR over the 53-year period is 13.3%. This is undoubtedly a striking figure, yet the UAE's growth is rarely highlighted by analysts versus other emerging markets like China, India and Singapore.

While the UAE has clearly benefited from its massive oil reserves, its economy is now considerably more diversified. In the first half of 2024, 75% of the country's GDP was derived from non-oil sectors.

Over the past few decades, it is also worth highlighting that Dubai has become a particularly important cultural, touristic and economic asset for the country, perhaps best illustrated by the city's Burj Khalifa, which is the world's tallest building. It's important not to underestimate the concerted effort which has been made to market the UAE to the world; Emirates airlines is owned by the government of Dubai and has both contributed to its status as a global transport hub as well as offered indirect promotion of the country through sponsorship deals, including of football teams like Arsenal and Real Madrid. Overall, these efforts have resulted in Dubai being the third most visited city in the world (based on international arrivals in 2023).

Historical Performance

When considering single country ETFs, historical performance can be a useful illustration of the potential results one can expect from owning a nation's equities over the long term, although clearly it is not an inevitable predictor of future performance.

In view of the striking growth figures outlined in the previous section, prospective investors in the UAE might expect that since its inception in April 2014, the fund tracking the country would have offered pretty impressive returns. Yet, the graph below reveals that over the eleven-year period since it was created it has offered a total return (dividends reinvested) of just 10.8%, equivalent to an average yearly return of less than 1%.

This means that from the fund's inception, an investor would have achieved more than 25x higher returns by simply owning an S&P 500 index fund. While this is a tough comparison considering the success of mega cap tech over the last decade (which the S&P is very focused on), it's also important to note that small cap US stocks beat the UAE ETF by more than 10x, and a strong European market (Switzerland) would have given you in excess of 9x the return from the UAE. Overall, the ETF holds up very poorly in terms of historical performance, as illustrated by these comparisons.

Holdings

The poor performance of the UAE's stock market, as represented by the ETF, suggests that it is not a very effective proxy for the performance of the broader Emirati economy. When observing this sort of behaviour in single country ETFs, the sectors held by the respective country funds are often to blame.

One reason which the ETF may have performed poorly is that its allocation towards energy (representing the oil and gas industry) is actually very low, at just 5%. Given that 25% of the UAE's GDP is derived from hydrocarbons, this tiny allocation towards energy seems to indicate that the ETF is poorly aligned with the UAE's broad economy.

Another observation is the massive concentration in the top three sectors, which together represent 76% of the ETF. While it is true that UAE is becoming an increasingly important global financial center, an almost 40% allocation towards financials does seem to expose investors to excessive risks from a single sector.

An additional consideration is that the tiny 1% allocation towards technology is a big contrast with the S&P 500, where 33% of holdings are technology companies. This may have also contributed to the ETFs extremely poor performance relative to the S&P over the past decade.

Considering sectors is somewhat useful, but the individual stocks held by the fund are even more relevant to prospective investors.

There is again a very high degree of concentration here, with the top ten holdings representing 72% of the ETF and the top three accounting for 43%. It is very important that investors recognize that given their dominance within the fund, the performance of these equities will in large part determine the performance of the ETF.

Given the heavy allocation towards the top three holdings, it is worth outlining them in further detail.

The largest holding is Emaar Properties with a weighting of 17%. This is a real estate development company which owns the Burj Khalifa, among other properties. It is very closely linked to the Dubai government, who are a major shareholder, which does seem to add in a degree of political risk, given that governments do not always act in the interests of outside shareholders.

The second-largest holding is First Abu Dhabi Bank, the largest bank in the UAE, accounting for 14% of the fund, and the third-largest holding is Emirates Telecommunications Group, with a weighting of almost 12%. As was the case with Emaar Properties, Emirates Telecommunications is also subject to a degree of government control, with a 60% ownership stake from the Emirates Investment Authority. Large government stakes are apparent in a number of other companies as one goes further down the list of holdings.

Dividends

Many income focused investors prefer foreign stocks on account of their relatively high dividends versus the U.S. market. While the UAE market does offer a dividend yield of almost 3%, which is considerably higher than the 1.29% offered by the S&P, I think there are better options in terms of dividend yield from other single country ETFs with less associated risk. iShares MSCI Mexico ETF (EWW), for example, which I covered in a recent article, offers a dividend yield of 3.36%, yet has offered much more competitive historical performance versus the S&P 500.

Fees

Another important consideration relating to the ETF are the expenses associated with owning it, and these are particularly relevant to long-term investors given the compounding effects associated with fees.

With an expense ratio of 0.60%, after 10 years, investors lose around 5.8% of their capital to fees, and after 20 years the figure is 11.3%. This may be tolerable for those who are desperate for exposure to the UAE, but for me, this figure doesn't seem particularly attractive.

Conclusion

Overall, while the UAE is an exciting economy, unfortunately this ETF which tracks its stock market is considerably less exciting, as demonstrated by its historical performance. I am concerned that the fund is excessively concentrated in just a few sectors and stocks and that these holdings don't represent a good proxy for the UAE's broader economy. Taken together with relatively high fees, I feel justified in assigning a sell rating to this ETF.

data.title: Teck Resources: All In On Copper
data.description:
Teck Resources (NYSE:TECK) stands at a turning point. While in the short term, the decline in margins and the growing costs are pressing, in the long term, its future depends on growing copper demand. The company is leaving its steelmaking coal business, freeing up some capital for future growth, has a strong balance sheet, and strategic projects (QB2, Zafranal), which could significantly increase production. Nevertheless, the current price ($37.40) already includes part of this optimism. Until profitability is recovered and projects do not start generating a tangible cash flow, I am rating HOLD.

Financials

Q1 2025 results show that the company is facing serious challenges. Compared to the same period last year, revenue decreased by as much as 33% from $2.38 billion to $1.59 billion. It is one of the weakest quarters since pandemic times. The gross profit decreased to 372.5 million and the profit margin fell to 23.4%. Two years ago, it was as high as 40-50%. This decline reflects weaker commodity prices and a surge in costs, which now consume roughly 77% of revenue. This indicates that the company is heavily influenced by inflation, expensive fuel, and labor.

Operating income was $310 million. By comparison, in December 2024, it only reached 184 million. This shows that Teck is somewhat recovering, although most of this is due to the reduced administrative and other operating costs rather than the growing main activity.

Net profit was around 257.1 million. Although this is a better result than Q3 2024, when the company suffered a major loss, the profit is still significantly lower than two years ago. The reason is the increased interest costs ($48.6 million) and exchange rate fluctuations.

EBITDA was $622 million, which is less than in 2022 when it exceeded $2 billion. This shows that the company's profitability is currently decreasing and might still and could stay under pressure. However, this level of EBITDA still allows maintaining a positive cash flow.

Macroeconomics

In the macroeconomic context, Teck's position is strategically favorable. The copper market for 2025 shows a possible deficit - the demand for refined copper is projected to exceed the supply. This imbalance is caused by supply interference in Latin America and the growing demand from the electric car and infrastructure sectors. It is also noteworthy that the demand for zinc in Europe and North America is weak; the Chinese recovery stabilizes prices. However, geopolitical risks grew, and I do not think this is over yet, especially among the US, China, and Canada. Teck has increased sales to India, Japan, and South Korea, reducing the dependence on China, which is still the largest part of the revenue currently, mitigating possible trade wars or escalations.

More than 20% of Teck's mined zinc production is sold to Chinese smelters, and while global demand for zinc and lead concentrates is strong, rerouting supplies from a mine as big as Red Dog could prove to be a major logistical challenge, according to the report.

Catalysts

First of all, the main catalyst is the completion of the Quebrada Blanca Phase 2 (QB2) project. This project, combined with others coming in, should increase copper production to 545-590k tons per year, which will allow Teck to become one of the world's largest independent copper producers. The second catalyst is the final investment decision of Zafranal (Peru) and San Nicolás (Mexico), planned in 2025 second half. If both projects are approved, they will add more than 180 thousand tons per year production from 2027. The third catalyst is the use of liquidity. After the sale of the coal unit, TECK has more than $5 billion in cash for special dividends, stock repurchases, or portfolio development. Any reports of these actions can act as short-term positive impulses for the share price.

Valuation

Currently, Teck shares are trading for around $37.40, which, based on basic financial indicators, can be considered close to fair value - neither too expensive nor very cheap. The current P/E is about 21.6. Compared to the closest competitors such as Hudbay (P/E ~ 13) or Material (P/E ~ 14.4), Teck looks a little more expensive but not too much.

If we were to consider that Teck should be invested in the same way as competitors (with P/E about 14), and the current earnings per share are approximately $1.87, then the correct price of the share would be about $26.18. In other words, investors are currently paying a little more, hoping that the profits will grow, or the market situation will improve. If we take the middle multiple - P/E about 18, the share price would be closer to $33.66, which would mean that the current price would already include a rather optimistic scenario.

In conclusion, Teck Resources currently seems to be priced fairly. The price has a growth potential if the prices of raw materials (especially copper) recover, but there is also a risk that the share price may fall to $26-30 if the results continue to deteriorate.

Risks

When investing in Teck Resources, it is necessary to assess several important risks that can affect the company's financial results and share price. The most important, is the cyclicality of commodity prices. As the main activity of the company is related to coal and copper extraction, its income is highly dependent on the prices of these raw materials. If the price of copper or coal falls due to a decline in demand or excessive supply, it directly reduces profit and investor confidence. It is especially important to pay attention to the growth rate of China, which has a great influence on the commodity market.

Second is the growth of operating costs. Although in Q1 2025, TECK showed some of the recovery signs, operating costs, including labor, fuel, and energy, now consume 77% of revenue, putting pressure on margins.

Third, regulatory and political risk. Teck has activities in several countries, including Canada, Chile, and Colombia, where environmental requirements, taxes, and permits are constantly changing. Political changes or protests against mining can pause projects, increase costs, or reduce production. This is especially relevant in Chile, where political uncertainty and mining tax reforms pose a risk to project economics

Summary

Teck Resources is currently valued fairly, so I give it a Hold rating. Although the company has long-term potential due to increasing copper demand and strategic projects (QB2, Zafranal, San Nicolás), current financial results indicate pressure, revenue not growing, margins shrinking, and expenses as much as 77% of income. Although EBITDA is still positive, the profitability is reduced, and P/E (21.6) indicates that optimism is already priced in.

Macroeconomics is currently favorable to copper, but geopolitical and regulatory dangers remain, especially in Latin America. In addition, the sale of the steelmaking coal unit will reduce short-term income, although it provides more liquidity.

Teck is valued neither too expensive nor cheap - $37.40, the price corresponds to the fair value, but does not provide a sufficient margin of safety for new investors. As a result, until the financial results show a strong recovery or the price of copper does not move up, this is not a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CCIF: The NAV Declines Continue (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

I've covered a few different CLO (collateralized loan obligation) funds recently, and they all seem to have taken a hit in valuation. Carlyle Credit Income Fund (NYSE:CCIF) seems to be no exception, as the price has declined significantly since the time of my last coverage. This higher interest rate environment has introduced some unique challenges for credit investments, so I wanted to revisit this fund to provide an updated look into its performance, valuation, and dividend sustainability. When I previously covered CCIF, I issued a buy rating due to its earnings resilience and dividend coverage during unfavorable conditions. CCIF recently reported its quarterly update, so I also wanted to take a look at how the earnings have shifted.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see how CCIF's price has deteriorated by about 17.5%. However, most of this decline seems to have been sparked by President Trump's tariff proposals that drove the markets downward at the beginning of April. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return is still at a loss of about 3%. The main appeal of holding a fund like CCIF is the generous starting dividend yield of about 19.3% and the monthly distributions. This makes CCIF an enticing choice for investors that want to prioritize building an income stream within their portfolio.

Interest rates have remained elevated longer than anticipated. This has made it a bit more difficult for CLO Equity investments to see their valuations increase. While the fund has been able to maintain thus far, it seems like interest rate cuts aren't likely to happen in the near term. Management has been proactive at trying to mitigate potential risks but I'm afraid the appeal of the fund is beginning to decrease. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that CCIF implements in order to generate its earnings.

Portfolio Strategy

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, CCIF has total investments at fair value of $197.9M that is spread across 1,990 different underlying loans within 61 different CLO holdings. After accounting for liabilities, CCIF's net assets have increased to $211.9M, which is a sizeable increase from the prior quarter's net assets of $191.1M. The large number of loans puts emphasis on the high level of diversity that the fund offers. For instance, exposure to the largest single obligor only accounts for 0.54% of the fund's total assets.

This focus on diversity is further expanded when looking at how spread out CCIF's investments are across different sectors. For instance, we can see that High Tech investments account for the largest portion of assets at 13%. This is followed by exposure to Healthcare and Finance related investments, accounting for 11% and 10% of assets respectively. The combination of these three sectors makes up approximately 34% of CCIF's total assets, so we can assume that these sectors will have the greatest impact on the fund's daily price movement and total return potential.

It's important to note that CCIF's portfolio is 99% comprised of CLO Equity investments. CLOs are essentially a pool of different loans that have been bundled together and broken down into different risk categories. CLO Equity investments are considered to be the riskiest because they sit at the bottom of the corporate capital structure and have the lowest priority for repayment. CLO Equity collects whatever cash flow is left after all the interest and loans payments have been paid out to the more senior tranches of the structure.

Therefore, these CLO Equity investments may not provide the most consistent levels of income for the fund. CCIF is able to participate in capital appreciation when the underlying borrower grows or when loans are completely repaid. As interest rates are elevated, the cost of debt is higher. This creates a more difficult environment for CCIF's borrowers to provide the fund managers with any excess or growth.

Q2 Earnings & Valuation

CCIF recently reported its Q2 earnings and the results were mixed in my opinion. Most notably, core net investment income amounted to $0.27 per share, which represents a slight uptick from last quarter's core net investment income of $0.26 per share. We can see how total investment income amounted to $8.5M, which was the highest level over the last twelve months. However, total expenses were also at its highest levels, which ultimately led to net investment income remaining mostly flat at $4M.

However, CCIF suffered from much larger net realized losses than usual, totaling a loss of $7.1M. This reduced the net income down to a loss of $3.1M for the quarter and caused both net investment income and recurring cash flows per share to shrink. Recurring cash flows per share landed at $0.49, which was a significant reduction from its prior levels over the last twelve months.

As a result of these losses, CCIF has now seen its NAV drastically decrease over the last few quarters. A decreasing NAV is one of the clearest indications that the fund is experiencing issues and may not be generating a sufficient level of earnings to support distributions or continued investment growth. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts:

  • Q2 2024: $7.88 per share
  • Q3 2024: $7.68 per share
  • Q4 2024: $7.64 per share
  • Q1 2025: $7.44 per share
  • Q2 2025: $6.98 per share

Despite the declining NAV, it seems like CCIF still trades at a premium to NAV valuation. CCIF currently trades at a slightly premium to NAV of about 1.9%. For reference, CCIF has traded at an average discount to NAV of about 1.88% over the last three-year period. So despite the NAV per share falling over the last few quarters, the price continues to trade at a higher premium than usual. From the recent performance, it seems like CCIF should be trading a much larger discount to NAV, but I suspect that the price to NAV valuation has remained a bit more resilient due to the fund's solid distribution coverage still and the fact that total assets have increased.

It's also noteworthy that CCIF's leverage has slightly increased from 0.33x up to 0.38x in the recent quarter. An increasing amount of leverage could potentially increase the risk profile for CCIF if investments do not perform as anticipated. Similarly, CCIF's total cash and equivalents have slightly decreased to $5.9M on hand, from the prior quarter's total of $6.4M.

Risk Profile

Out of all of the loans within CCIF, the fund has a weighted average loan rating of B+. For reference, anything rated under a BBB is considered to be below investment grade. Therefore, CCIF is primarily focused on riskiest borrowers and increases the risk profile to higher interest rates. Borrowers that have poor credit ratings typically have less consistent cash flows, may be more vulnerable to shifts in consumer spending, and may be more likely to default on their loans when market headwinds occur.

This is why we can see CCIF shares an inverse relationship with the federal funds rate below. When interest rates were cut to near zero levels throughout 2020 and 2021, CCIF's price rapidly increased as it was a lot easier for borrowers to make efficient use of their debt capital. Conversely, CCIF's price rapidly deteriorated as interest rates approached their decade high throughout 2022 and 2023. A higher cost of debt makes it more costly to hold debt on the balance sheet, as borrowers need to allocate more capital towards interest expenses.

The potential for higher defaults could continue to suppress CCIF's price throughout the remainder of the year. Management stated that their loan to maturity default rate sits at 1.3%. It seems like management is also putting in the effort to research how tariffs will impact their portfolio over time.

CCIF's loan portfolio has experienced an LTM default rate of 1.3%, inclusive of out-of-court restructurings, which is approximately one-third of the market rate as we continue to leverage our in-house credit expertise from over 20 U.S. credit analysts to complete bottoms-up fundamental analysis on the underlying portfolios. Over the past few months, we've leveraged the insights of Carlyle's in-house global research, government affairs and the investment teams to assess the impact of tariffs. While the situation continues to evolve, we believe the CLO market is well positioned to navigate this environment, given the diversified nature of the underlying loan portfolios, ample market liquidity and the resilience of loan borrowers that they've demonstrated over the past two years in the face of elevated interest rates. - Lauren Basmadjian, Chairman

S&P Global estimates that defaults could rise to 1.6% by December 2025 in its base scenario. This would be caused by slowing economic growth and rising prices that create headwinds for borrowers. However, I believe that we are much closer to the pessimistic scenario where defaults could increase as high as 3% by December 2025, which would be caused by an escalation in tariffs and higher inflationary data. Higher inflationary data could cause the Fed to leave rates unchanged for the rest of the year.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.105 per share, the current dividend yield sits as high as 19.3%. If we multiply this monthly distribution by three, we get a quarterly payout amount of $0.315 per share. As previously mentioned, recurring cash flows per share amounted to $0.49 for the quarter. Even though this was a sizeable decrease from its prior quarterly earnings amounts, this still represents a healthy distribution coverage rate of approximately 155%.

Therefore, I wouldn't be worried about a distribution cut yet, since recurring cash flows still offer a healthy level of excess coverage. So from an income perspective, investors may very well be able to continue collecting a high rate of income for the remainder of 2025. I just wouldn't have any expectations for supplementals to be paid out during the remainder of the year. CCIF was also able to allocate $30.3M towards new CLO investments during the quarter. If these investments are efficient, we can see earnings improve once the new investments start to materialize.

It's also worth noting that the distributions received from CCIF are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have the most unfavorable tax consequences that investors will need to be prepared for. Based on the amount of capital invested, your specific tax bracket, and desired income levels, a position in CCIF may be best utilized within a tax advantaged account to offset these potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on CCIF to a hold for now. Although the fund has maintained a healthy distribution coverage, the earnings and recurring cash flows have experienced a sizeable decline. Additionally, the fund's NAV has continued to slide for more than four consecutive quarters at this point, which is an indication that the underlying strategy is feeling the weight of higher interest rates. As long as interest rates remain elevated, I do not think CCIF will experience a positive growth momentum shift. While these issues may not be a reason to completely sell out of a position, they also don't indicate that a turnaround will be happening yet.

data.title: Salesforce: A Steal For Growth Investors
data.description:
Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) submitted stronger-than-expected earnings for its first fiscal quarter due to strength in AI and, specifically, Agentforce. The CRM applications provider generated 8% top line growth in Q1'26, on a year-over-year basis and generated a ton of free cash flow. Salesforce returned more than $3.0B in capital to investors in the first-quarter, mainly through stock buybacks. Shares remain widely undervalued based off of earnings and are a steal for growth investors, in my opinion.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Salesforce a strong buy in my last work -- AI Catalyst Is Kicking Off -- due to the success Salesforce has had deploying AI technology through its Agentforce offering to enterprise customers. In the first-quarter, the CRM applications provider continued to do well with Agentforce and continued to generate a significant amount of free cash flow, at very healthy FCF margins. The SaaS provider also raised its top-line outlook for the current fiscal year, and I believe the company's shares are widely undervalued based off of earnings.

Salesforce beats Q1’26 estimates

Salesforce topped bottom and top-line estimates for the first fiscal quarter: the CRM applications provider had adjusted earnings of $2.58 per-share, which beat the consensus estimate by $0.03 per-share. Revenues came in at $9.83B, beating the estimate by $80M.

Salesforce generated revenue of $9.8B in the first fiscal quarter, showing a solid 8% growth, and the company’s revenue performance was chiefly carried by two segments: Integration & Analytics and Platform which are helping companies gain business insights and build cloud-based applications. In these two segments, Salesforce saw 10% and 14% growth on a year-over-year basis.

One key driver of growth for the CRM applications provider is Agentforce which is a service that allows enterprise customers to build, customize, and deploy AI agents at scale. Since launch in October 2024, the software company has signed more than 8,000 deals with customers, and Salesforce added 3,000 new deals since its last earnings report for the January quarter.

Salesforce is widely profitable, on both a gross profit and free cash flow basis, which in my opinion makes the software company a low-risk investment choice for growth investors. In the last three years, Salesforce's gross profit grew a solid 35% and the company is achieving high gross margins as well. In Q1'26, the company's GAAP gross margin was 77.0% compared to 76.3% in the year-earlier period.

In terms of free cash flow, Salesforce also had a very strong quarter, which is quite typical for the SaaS industry as customers tend to sign new software contracts and renew their licenses in either Q4 or Q1. Salesforce generated $6.3B in free cash flow on revenue of $9.8B, which calculates to a massive free cash flow margin of 64%. In the trailing-twelve months period, Salesforce generated free cash flow of $12.6B, which calculates to an impressive 32.8% margin. Because of Salesforce's enormous free cash flow power, the software enterprise returned a ton of cash to shareholders in the form of buybacks.

In the first-quarter of FY 2026, Salesforce returned $3.1B in total, in the form of both dividends and stock buybacks to shareholders, which represented a free cash flow return percentage of 49% which was about 32% above the previous four-quarter average. The majority of capital returns (87%) occurred in terms of stock buybacks.

In order to accelerate its AI strategy, Salesforce announced last week that it will acquire Informatica, a cloud data management company, for $8.0B. Since Informatica itself has heavily invested in artificial intelligence capabilities, Salesforce will be able to use the company's technology to support its own AI-related growth ambitions, especially with regard to high-potential products like Agentforce. Last year, Salesforce acquired Own Company for $1.9B, which also had a core operational focus on data management solutions. With Salesforce obviously having a ton of free cash flow at its disposal, I would expect more acquisitions in the AI realm going forward.

Salesforce’s outlook for FY 2026

Salesforce raised its revenue guidance for the current fiscal year by $450M, at the mid-point, amid strong AI product up-take and success with the company’s Agentforce enrollment. The software company now expects to generate 8-9% top-line growth in FY 2026 and bring in $41.0-41.3B in revenue this year.

Salesforce’s valuation

Shares of Salesforce are currently valued at a very reasonable forward price-to-earnings ratio of 21.1X, which is slightly (6%) below the 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio of 22.4X. The industry group -- which includes Palantir Technologies (PLTR), Microsoft (MSFT) and SAP (SAP) -- has a forward (FY 2026) P/E ratio of 63.6X... which is due mainly because of Palantir. The AI analytics company has seen a rapid valuation upswing in the last twelve months due to the success of Palantir AI, known as AIP, which is the company's main AI product. While I consider Palantir to be massively overvalued based off of earnings, I believe that Salesforce is way too cheap.

With an estimated long term EPS growth rate of 19%, Salesforce is expected to see strong earnings growth in the years ahead. Palantir is expected to boost its earnings by 31% annually going forward, but the AI analytics company really does not have that big of a growth advantage over Salesforce.

In my opinion, Salesforce could trade at a forward P/E ratio of ~25X given its strong market position in the CRM applications market, its enormous free cash flow power and its focus on returning cash flow to shareholders. A 25X P/E ratio implies, based off of a consensus earnings figure of $12.66 per-share, implies a fair value in the neighborhood of $317 per-share, implying 19% upside revaluation potential (not including return potential linked to the company's dividend).

Risks with Salesforce

The biggest risk for CRM applications providers focusing on AI tools is that other companies could emerge with stronger AI capabilities. In today’s world, a start-up with just three people can leverage AI technology to compete with the largest firms, if it can provide a better product. By lowering entry barriers, I believe moats in the software market have generally diminished, which exposes companies like Salesforce to stronger competitive pressures. What would change my mind about Salesforce is if the company were to see weaker free cash flow margins and falling adoption/retention in Agentforce.

Final thoughts

Salesforce is growing rapidly and seeing significant product uptake for its AI- centered product offering Agentforce which is helping enterprise customers deploy AI agents autonomously. What I like about Salesforce is that the SaaS enterprise is highly profitable, in terms of earnings and free cash flow growth, and the CRM application provider returns a ton of cash to shareholders. Agentforce-related growth is impressive, and so is the company's commitment to buying back more shares. Salesforce's shares are very attractively valued on the drop and have double-digit revaluation potential.

data.title: Costco: Q3 Results Point To Growth Acceleration Ahead (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Costco (COST) has performed more or less in-line with the S&P500 since my last update:

So I think my 'Neutral/Hold' view has been an accurate one.

Thesis

The company reported its Q3 FY25 earnings (the company has an Aug-ending fiscal year) yesterday and after a review of their results and commentary, I am becoming more bullish on COST stock:

  1. Lower commodity prices provides Costco an opportunity to strengthen its market position
  2. Costco is investing in capacity to encourage and handle higher traffic
  3. Valuations are relatively lower than usual vs comps
  4. COST's technicals vs SPX500 look good
  5. Tariffs are a monitorable risk, but the data points to a better than expected situation so far

Lower Commodity Prices Provide Costco An Opportunity To Strengthen Its Market Position

Costco noted that they have been seeing a drop in key commodity prices, for which they are passing the bulk of the benefits back to their customers. This is strengthening their market position:

We have been very fortunate with some of the key commodities coming down and then our buyers are immediately the first ones down in those goods. So we take every advantage of every opportunity that we can lower prices... And we've seen our competitive landscape improve slightly at the latter part of the quarter, which is very good for us. So it means that we're doing the right thing. But we're going to continue to invest in price.

- CEO Ron Vachris in the Q3 FY25 earnings call

The size of the pricing drops in some of Costco's key food categories was meaningful:

we were able to lower egg prices by approximately 10% and butter by $1 per cell unit or approximately 7%, while also returning margins to more normal levels... In addition to lowering prices on butter and eggs, a few other examples where we were able to lower prices in the quarter to provide greater value to our members included 2-liter Kirkland Signature organic extra virgin olive oil from $24.99 to $18.39 [26% reduction], Kirkland Signature Chocolate Macadamia Clusters from $17.99 to $14.69 [18% reduction] and Kirkland Signature Organic Mixed Nut Butter from $8.69 to $7.59 [13% reduction].

- CFO Gary Millerchip in the Q3 FY25 earnings call

Relevant agricultural commodities' prices outlook for 2025 and 2026 suggests that we can expect further easing of Costco's input costs, which will be passed onto customers:

I expect this to contribute to continued share gains for Costco. Also, note that margin expansion still possible as Costco does tend to pocket some (~10%) of the lower costs to improve their bottom line gradually over time:

our philosophy is how do we take 90% of that value and give it back to the member to drive top line. And we do think, over time, we can still be increasing our margin a little bit as part of that plan.

- CFO Gary Millerchip in the Q3 FY25 earnings call

Overall, I think AI-driven opex efficiencies can further boost TTM EBIT margins to north of 4% in a sustainable way over the next few quarters.

Costco Is Investing In Capacity To Encourage And Handle Higher Traffic

Costco has seen steady mid-high single digit YoY traffic growth worldwide (blue line in the chart below):

In Q4 FY25, the company plans on opening 10 more warehouses around the world, bringing the total net count to 914:

We plan to open another 10 warehouses during the fourth fiscal quarter, which will include our second warehouse in Sweden, our 20th warehouse in Korea and our 110th warehouse in Canada.

- CFO Gary Millerchip in the Q3 FY25 earnings call

80% of these new warehouse openings are going to be placed close to ~40 high-volume (more than $400 million in annual sales) existing warehouses. Although this will lead to some cannibalization, the idea is to ease traffic flow and improve member experience, which can ultimately lead to a net gain in traffic overall. This would complement its recent move to extend hours of its gas stations, which has already improved gasoline sale volumes:

…it is a strategic priority for all of us in the company right now is to continue to how we improve the member experience in our high-volume warehouses. As we speak about the fourth quarter openings, about 80% of those warehouses we're opening are going to cannibalize high-volume locations for us that's going to take some relief off. So we strategically look at new markets for openings, but with a real importance on strategically cannibalizing those warehouses where we can make that improvement of member experience in there as well. The recent expansion of gasoline hours was a great indicator that the throughput for our members improved nicely, and we saw that immediately in gallon increases.

- CEO Ron Vachris in the Q3 FY25 earnings call

With these initiatives, I think we can expect to see overall TTM revenue acceleration inch up closer to 10% YoY:

Valuations Are Relatively Lower Than Usual Vs. Comps

Costco is trading at a 52.0x 1-yr fwd PE; almost triple the median 17.3x of its comps:

Now, a premium is absolutely justified because Costco's superior business model has led to it outperforming its peers by a very wide margin when it comes to total return wealth generation:

And the current ~200% premium vs comps is actually a little lower than the typical 227% premium that COST has commanded over its peers:

Hence, I conclude that the valuations look acceptable today, especially given the accelerated growth prospects ahead.

COST's Technicals Vs. SPX500 Look Good

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of COST vs. SPX500

COST vs. SPX500 is bouncing off a key support level and printing a bullish engulfing candle. I think this is a good sign that further upside can follow suit.

Tariffs Are A Monitorable Risk, But The Data Points To A Better Than Expected Situation So Far

Tariff developments are incredibly volatile and have low-predictability, with the situation changing day-by-day. For example, just 2 days ago, the U.S. Court of International Trade ruled that Trump exceeded presidential authority when the President imposed reciprocal tariffs on the world. This stopped tariff collection immediately. But just yesterday, this changed as the US was allowed to keep collecting tariffs whilst an appeals process against the ruling goes on.

My way of handling this extreme volatility is to calmly just respond to the container import volume data:

TEU stands for Twenty-food Equivalent Unit; a standard size of a shipping container used to transport most merchandise

As you can see from the chart above, the US Container import volume data shows us that despite all the noise around tariffs, import volumes are tracking rather well this year compared to the past 4 years. Month on month [MoM], import volumes have on aggregate increased 2.8%:

So whilst tariffs remain a risk to monitor, I am not so concerned about them increasing input costs materially right now. If you're interested, in my last article on Costco, I delved deeper into the impact of tariffs on the company. In any case, I think lower agricultural commodity prices can mitigate the impact of higher import costs.

Takeaway & Positioning

I am becoming more bullish on Costco after its Q3 FY25 earnings release. I think the tailwind of lower commodity prices gives an opportunity for Costco to strengthen its market position and steadily improve margins as well. I also expect to see some growth acceleration, fueled by net traffic increases arising from new warehouse openings. On the valuations side, Costco trades at a well-deserved premium to its comps, but encouragingly, the current premium is a bit lower than usual. The relative technicals on the COST vs SPX500 charts also look bullish as the ratio prices bounce off a key support level in a convincing way.

Tariffs remain a very volatile monitorable risk, that can put pressure on input costs. But I am not so concerned about this right now as the data suggests that import volumes remain rather healthy, and a favorable agricultural commodities' pricing outlook can help mitigate some impacts of higher import costs.

Rating: 'Buy'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: Weekly Commentary: TACO And Dumpling
data.description:
The S&P 500 traded within 3% of February's record high in Thursday trading. To be sure, the rally off April lows has been nothing short of spectacular. Like the "old days" (aka September to February). Nvidia. MAG7. Manic AI and crypto. Heck, the Broker/Dealer Index traded to record highs a week ago. Junk bond and leveraged loan prices have recovered along with equities. The month saw a record ($153bn) month of May investment-grade bond issuance. Financial conditions have loosened significantly.

Looks too much like a set-up. Risk markets have quickly settled back to their baseline: disregard myriad risks until one strikes between the eyes. Squeeze the shorts. Buy the dips like you mean it - with options, of course, offering the best bang for the buck. As they have throughout history, speculative Bubbles are just phenomenal at ensuring no one is spared.

My concerns have not been assuaged. If anything, there is ongoing confirmation of my analytical thesis. I see an equities Bubble incapable of orderly adjustment, which only boosts odds of "disorderly" - market disorder beyond last month and last August. Quick policy responses reversed both nascent de-leveraging episodes. But all the speculative leverage remains - festering and susceptible. Meanwhile, looming economic and Credit Cycle days of reckoning are closing in.

Extraordinary late-cycle resilience is only fitting of history's greatest Bubble. Explanations include Credit system might that comes from steadfast $2 TN or so of annual federal debt growth - the reckless expansion of money-like Treasury debt. What's more, Trillions of Treasury/Agency market speculative leverage create a hardier market structure than, say, levered positions in more suspect corporate debt or risky mortgages. And it's worth noting the recent comments of the robustness of private Credit and private markets more generally.

Indeed, April's instability and quick recovery showcased the trumpeted "private market" advantage of being insulated from the whims of the public marketplace. No need to fret volatility, sinking market prices or illiquidity - for a marketplace that doesn't do a lot of asset pricing or trading.

In another extraordinary late-cycle Bubble twist, some of the most levered players are making money hand over fist from booming market trading.

May 27 - Bloomberg (Katherine Doherty and Carmen Arroyo): "Citadel Securities reported record profit and trading revenue in the first three months of the year, buoyed by market volatility that's continued since President Donald Trump took office. The market-making giant posted $3.4 billion in net trading revenue in the first three months of the year, up roughly 45% from the same period last year… Net income surged 70% to $1.7 billion, the people said… The firm took in $9.7 billion in net trading revenue last year, its largest haul since the company was founded in 2002."

One of these days, deleveraging will careen from "nascent" - easily rectified by timely policy responses - to a full-fledged market crisis posing extreme challenges for global policymakers. For starters, if hedge funds (massive levered "basis trades" and such) panic in concert with foreign holders (i.e., Asian insurance companies and levered institutions), buyers would need to materialize for potentially Trillions of Treasury and debt securities. Such a liquidity challenge would blow holes in critical derivatives market assumptions (liquid and continuous markets), along with premises for scores of investment strategies. It would unmask major structural shortcomings throughout the "private markets."

In short, structural issues these days create acute vulnerability to a spiraling crisis of confidence in Treasuries, derivatives, risk markets and the dollar.

Market rallies, tariff pauses, and China deals and "resets" notwithstanding, the Trump administration presents clear and present market turmoil risk. My worries of trade wars and worse with superpower rival China were not allayed by the Geneva "deal" or the President's talk of "total reset". I assumed it was a mere tactical retreat by an administration petrified by market fragilities and mounting economic risks. I doubted there would be any softening of Beijing's resolve to "fight to the end" in an ongoing test of wills between fierce adversaries battling on multiple fronts.

I did not expect President Trump would respond kindly to the outpouring of scorn and mockery (at home and abroad), of what the Wall Street Journal editorial board called the "The Great Trump Tariff Rollback." "'Trump Chickens Out' Goes Viral In China Over Tariffs!"

From the Financial Times' Robert Armstrong: "Can I just say one very important thing, which is, you know, obviously I didn't expect this to happen, and the outcome I really, really hope does not happen is that this has anything to do with the President stopping his habitual chickening out. Let us state clearly, chickening out is good and something to be celebrated. Bad policy chickening out, hooray. So, this is an unintended consequences thing if, as I think is quite unlikely by the way, given that I am an unimportant person and the President is an important person, if this gets into his head and he digs in his heels about some of this stuff. That is really a disaster for which I am very, very sorry."

"Quite likely" would be a better bet. Robert Armstrong coined TACO - Trump Always Chickens Out - in a piece discussing Wall Street traders keen to wager on the President's propensity to lose his nerve. The memes and media attention can't be sitting well in the Oval Office.

May 28 - Associated Press (Josh Boak): "President Donald Trump wants the world to know he's no 'chicken' just because he's repeatedly backed off high tariff threats. The U.S. Republican president's tendency to levy extremely high import taxes and then retreat has created what's known as the 'TACO' trade… Markets generally sell off when Trump makes his tariff threats and then recover after he backs down. Trump was visibly offended when asked about the phrase… and rejected the idea that he's 'chickening out,' saying that the reporter's inquiry was 'nasty.' 'You call that chickening out?' Trump said. 'It's called negotiation,' adding that he sets a 'ridiculous high number and I go down a little bit, you know, a little bit' until the figure is more reasonable."

Concluding his rambling response, the President scolded the reporter: "Don't ever say what you said. That's a nasty question. Go ahead. To me, that's the nastiest question." My thoughts returned to the September Trump/Harris debate, when the President should have laughed it off and not taken the bait: "People start leaving his rallies early out of exhaustion and boredom."

The President absolutely revels in being the world's most powerful specimen. Aspirations to cozy up to Putin, Xi and the Nobel Peace Prize are slipping away. I suspect he will struggle mightily adjusting to waning power, much less jeers and taunts. "Trump Warns Putin is 'Playing With Fire' After Declaring the Russian President has 'Gone Absolutely CRAZY'." "Kremlin Muses About 'Emotional Overload' After Trump Asks if Putin is 'Crazy.'" "Iran Calls Trump Wish for Deal That Lets US 'Blow Up' Nuclear Sites a Fantasy." "'Sleazebag': Trump Attacks Former Federalist Society Chair Over Court's Tariff Ruling." "Trump Declares War on His Own Judicial Legacy." "Supreme Court Walks a Tightrope as it Confronts Trump's Power Moves."

President Trump (Truth Social 5/30/25): "Two weeks ago China was in grave economic danger! The very high Tariffs I set made it virtually impossible for China to TRADE into the United States marketplace which is, by far, number one in the World. We went, in effect, COLD TURKEY with China, and it was devastating for them. Many factories closed and there was, to put it mildly, 'civil unrest.' I saw what was happening and didn't like it, for them, not for us. I made a FAST DEAL with China in order to save them from what I thought was going to be a very bad situation, and I didn't want to see that happen. Because of this deal, everything quickly stabilized and China got back to business as usual. Everybody was happy! That is the good news!!! The bad news is that China, perhaps not surprisingly to some, HAS TOTALLY VIOLATED ITS AGREEMENT WITH US. So much for being Mr. NICE GUY!"

Drudge: "Trump Serves Taco Friday! Talks 'Tough on China.' Wall St Yawns."

Wall Street may yawn, but in Beijing, the President's comments are fighting words. Invoking "civil unrest" was no accident. Few issues carry such sensitivity for Xi Jinping and the Chinese communist party. It's a disturbing post. Neither the tone nor content will be well-received by the Chinese. Both are disrespectful and threatening. Ain't Gonna Work.

To the reasonably well-informed, the post lacks all credibility to the point of bordering on irrationality. Caved "for them, not for us"? And I fear this is the type of lashing out we can expect from a President agitated by - and compelled to aggressively respond to - a world skeptical of his power and fortitude. In today's tinderbox geopolitical landscape, it's a dangerous dynamic.

May 30 - Bloomberg (Jennifer A. Dlouhy): "US President Donald Trump expressed confidence a talk with Chinese President Xi Jinping could ease fresh trade tensions, after White House officials vented anger over Beijing's pace of issuing promised export licenses. The dust-up threatened to again upend trade relations between the world's two largest economies, which have been held together by a fragile, weeks-old tariff truce. 'They violated a big part of the agreement we made,' Trump told reporters Friday... 'But I'm sure that I'll speak to President Xi, and hopefully we'll work that out.'"

For Beijing, it's surely "those who live in glass houses…" This week's developments wouldn't seem to encourage Xi Jinping to pick up the phone.

May 29 - New York Times (Keith Bradsher): "After stepping back this month from an escalating and dangerous war of tariffs, the United States and China are now threatening to undermine their uneasy truce. On May 12, the countries announced after weekend meetings in Geneva that they would suspend most of their recently imposed tariffs. Since then, however, both governments have shown that they are still prepared to wield controls over critical exports as weapons against one another, with moves that are potentially even more damaging to trade and global supply chains. China has restricted its exports of rare earth magnets, which are crucial for cars, semiconductors, aircraft and many other applications. Close to 90% of the world's rare earth metals, including magnets, are produced in China. And the United States on May 13 banned the latest semiconductors from Huawei, a Chinese electronics giant. Then on Wednesday, President Trump suspended the shipment of American semiconductors and some aerospace equipment needed for China's commercial aircraft, the C919, a signature project in China's push toward economic self-reliance."

May 29 - Bloomberg: "The US plans to start 'aggressively' revoking visas for Chinese students, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said, escalating the Trump administration's push for greater scrutiny of foreigners attending American universities. Rubio said… students affected would include 'those with connections to the Chinese Communist Party or studying in critical fields.' The US will also enhance scrutiny 'of all future visa applications from the People's Republic of China and Hong Kong,' he added… Chinese Foreign Ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning accused the US of taking its decision 'under the pretext of ideology and national security'…, adding that it would harm people-to-people relations. 'Such a politicized and discriminatory move lays bare the US lie behind the so-called freedom and openness that the US touts… It will only further undermine its image in the world and national reputation'."

The administration's move against Chinese students is an alarming escalation. Perhaps it's a trade negotiation ploy that will be reversed. But throwing 270,000 existing - and many thousands of prospective - students' futures into turmoil is not damage (to students, families, and universities) easily rectified. For me, it signaled a hard-line approach in the middle of already fraught negotiations that, following a ratcheting up of export controls, will stoke only stronger resolve from Beijing and the Chinese people. As for Chinese nationalism and Xi's domestic popularity, Trump is the ultimate gift that keeps giving.

The Geneva "total reset" was merely an offramp allowing the resumption of (higher priced) Chinese exports to the U.S. - with little expectation in Washington for a resolution to trade and other critical issues with Beijing. It's difficult not to be pessimistic. The unfolding U.S./China trade war remains a major problem, with especially broad ramifications, knowing the President will be determined to quash all the TACO talk.

The U.S./China trade "deal" quieted April's fears of Chinese Treasury selling. Hardly missing a beat, concerns shifted to Taiwan and Japan.

May 29 - Financial Times (Bertrand Benoit): "A closely watched auction of 40-year Japanese government debt has drawn the lowest demand in 10 months as concerns mount over the world's third-biggest bond market. Demand for the government's offer on Wednesday of about $3.5bn of 40-year notes attracted a bid-to-cover ratio… of 2.2, the lowest level since July 2024 and a reflection of what some traders have called a 'buyers' strike' among Japanese life insurers and other domestic participants… If this had been a Japan-only story then it might be easy to ignore, but the JGB sell-off was a major factor in the recent global duration wobble, and today's renewed weakness seems to once again be infecting the long end of the yield curve pretty much everywhere. The Japanese government bond market is one of the largest in the world, so it has always exerted some influence elsewhere. The Bank of Japan's 'yield curve control' programme certainly seems to have acted as an anchor for global bond yields for much of the past decade. But the way JGBs now seem to be actively driving other developed bond markets is interesting. As Ajay Rajadhyaksha wrote on FTAV last week, the core problem is an acute imbalance between supply and demand."

May 28 - CNBC (Lee Ying Shan): "Japan's 40-year government bonds yields hit an all-time high of 3.689% Thursday… Yields on 30-year government debt are up more than 60 bps this year at 2.914%, also not too far from all-time highs… Higher Japan government bond yields could spark a wave of capital repatriation with Japanese investors pulling funds from the U.S. There could be a 'trigger point' where Japan's investors suddenly move their capital from the U.S. back home, Macquarie's analysts said… Should Japanese government bond yields continue to climb, the move could 'trigger a global financial market Armageddon,' said Albert Edwards, global strategist at Societe Generale… As higher yields strengthen the yen, it will impact domestic appetite to invest abroad… With the Bank of Japan scaling back bond purchases in a seminal monetary policy shift last year, and private players not stepping up, the demand-supply mismatch is likely to fuel higher yields. 'If sharply higher JGB yields entice Japanese investors to return home, the unwinding of the carry trade could cause a loud sucking sound in U.S. financial assets,' Edwards said."

May 27 - Reuters (Takaya Yamaguchi and Leika Kihara): "Japan will consider trimming issuance of super-long bonds in the wake of recent sharp rises in yields for the notes, two sources told Reuters…, as policymakers seek to soothe market concerns about worsening government finances. Super-long bond yields slumped on the report, pushing down the Japanese yen and U.S. Treasury yields along the way, as markets cheered Tokyo's readiness to arrest spikes in long-term interest rates."

Ending the week down 11 bps at 4.40%, one could have missed that 10-year Treasury yields traded at 4.54% Thursday morning following the rough 40-year Japanese bond auction. JGBs, Treasuries and global bonds remain vulnerable. By the end of the week, signals that Japan would cut the size of long-bond issuance had done the trick - for JGBs, Treasuries, derivative "swaps," and global bonds more generally. Curiously, the dollar rallied versus the yen on the news. Bond, currency, and swaps markets seem these days one big, highly correlated, volatile, and fragile (highly levered) "trade".

On the subject of elevated market yields - and in the context of looming economic and Credit Cycle days of reckoning noted above - it's time to keep a watchful eye on increasingly vulnerable housing markets. Pricing data from earlier in the week were noteworthy. The FHFA House Price Index (March data) posted a weaker-than-expected 0.1% decline, the first negative print since August 2022. S&P CoreLogic (March) prices were reported at a much weaker-than-expected negative 0.12%, the first decline since January 2023.

May 30 - Bloomberg (Conor Sen): "House prices are falling, and it's no longer just a Florida and Texas story. Rising inventory across the country and still reluctant buyers mean that those looking to sell face the prospect of more competition and lower prices next spring if they don't close on a deal soon. For buyers, holding out can mean a better price. This shift in market psychology should finally break the impasse that has choked transactions for the past few years… But the enormous cost of homeownership has continued to turn off buyers, leading to a steady grind higher in the number of existing homes for sale. These increased 40% over the past two years and, in April, were at the highest level since 2020. There are now nearly 500,000 more home sellers than buyers, according to Redfin Corp., the biggest differential since the company began tracking the data in 2013."

For the Week:

The S&P 500 rallied 1.9% (up 0.5% y-t-d), and the Dow gained 1.6% (down 0.6%). The Utilities increased 1.3% (up 8.2%). The Banks gained 1.5% (unchanged), and the Broker/Dealers added 1.2% (up 13.9%). The Transports advanced 1.0% (down 7.6%). The S&P 400 Midcaps increased 0.8% (down 3.8%), and the small cap Russell 2000 gained 1.3% (down 7.3%). The Nasdaq 100 rose 2.0% (up 1.6%). The Semiconductors recovered 1.2% (down 4.5%). The Biotechs gained 0.8% (down 2.8%). While bullion dipped $23, the HUI gold index was little changed (up 44.5%).

Three-month Treasury bill rates ended the week at 4.23%. Two-year government yields fell 10 bps to 3.90% (down 34bps y-t-d). Five-year T-note yields dropped 12 bps to 3.96% (down 42bps). Ten-year Treasury yields fell 11 bps to 4.40% (down 17bps). Long bond yields dropped 11 bps to 4.93% (up 15bps). Benchmark Fannie Mae MBS yields sank 14 bps to 5.73% (down 11bps).

Italian 10-year yields dropped 10 bps to 3.48% (down 4bps y-t-d). Greek 10-year yields fell eight bps to 3.24% (up 2bps). Spain's 10-year yields dropped 10 bps to 3.09% (up 3bps). German bund yields fell seven bps to 2.50% (up 13bps). French yields dropped 10 bps to 3.16% (down 3bps). The French to German 10-year bond spread narrowed three to 66 bps. U.K. 10-year gilt yields slipped three bps to 4.65% (up 8bps). U.K.'s FTSE equities index added 0.6% (up 7.3% y-t-d).

Japan's Nikkei 225 Equities Index rallied 2.2% (down 4.8% y-t-d). Japanese 10-year "JGB" yields declined three bps to 1.50% (up 40bps y-t-d). France's CAC40 added 0.2% (up 5.0%). The German DAX equities index gained 1.6% (up 20.5%). Spain's IBEX 35 equities index increased 0.3% (up 22.1%). Italy's FTSE MIB index rose 1.6% (up 17.3%). EM equities were mixed. Brazil's Bovespa index dipped 0.6% (up 13.9%), and Mexico's Bolsa index fell 1.0% (up 16.8%). South Korea's Kospi rallied 4.1% (up 12.4%). India's Sensex equities index slipped 0.3% (up 3.7%). China's Shanghai Exchange Index was unchanged (unchanged). Turkey's Borsa Istanbul National 100 index dropped 3.6% (down 8.2%).

Federal Reserve Credit slipped $6.4 billion last week to $6.637 TN. Fed Credit was down $2.252 TN from the June 22, 2022, peak. Over the past 298 weeks, Fed Credit expanded $2.911 TN, or 78%. Fed Credit inflated $3.826 TN, or 136%, over the past 655 weeks. Elsewhere, Fed holdings for foreign owners of Treasury, Agency Debt recovered $7.2 billion last week to $3.260 TN. "Custody holdings" were down $62 billion y-o-y, or 1.1%.

Total money market fund assets declined $19.8 billion to $6.949 TN. Money funds were up $883 billion, or 14.6% y-o-y.

Total Commercial Paper gained $6.3 billion to a new 16-year high $1.449 TN. CP has expanded $361 billion y-t-d and $169 billion, or 13.2%, y-o-y.

Freddie Mac 30-year fixed mortgage rates increased three bps this week to a four-month high 6.89% (down 14bps y-o-y). Fifteen-year rates added two bps to 6.03% (down 33bps). Bankrate's survey of jumbo mortgage borrowing costs had 30-year fixed rates up three bps to 7.02% (down 39bps).

Currency Watch:

May 27 - Bloomberg: "The dollar's extended slide has prompted China's central bank to change tack in managing its currency, as it pivots from supporting the yuan to guarding against the risk of a rapid appreciation. The People's Bank of China fixed the yuan's daily reference rate at a slightly weaker level than market forecasts on Monday and Tuesday, after setting it stronger for most of the past six months."

For the week, the U.S. Dollar Index increased 0.2% to 99.329 (down 8.4% y-t-d). For the week on the downside, the Brazilian real declined 1.3%, the South Korean won 1.2%, the Mexican peso 1.0%, the Japanese yen 1.0%, the Norwegian krone 0.9%, the South African rand 0.9%, the Australian dollar 0.9%, the Swedish krona 0.7%, the British pound 0.6%, the Singapore dollar 0.5%, the New Zealand dollar 0.4%, the Swiss franc 0.2%, the euro 0.1%, and the Canadian dollar 0.1%. The Chinese (onshore) renminbi declined 0.25% versus the dollar (up 1.39% y-t-d).
