data.title: If Not One Thing, Then Another
data.description:
Keeping up with the financial news is a tall order in this Year 2025 of the Common Era, which will almost certainly enter the logbooks of history as the Year of Uncertainty. Consider the week that is ending today. It started off, well, just fine, as far as things go these days.

Those nasty 50 percent tariffs on goods imported from the European Union that the administration had announced just last Friday were, surprise of all surprises, delayed from a June 2 commencement to July 9. That nice little Memorial Day weekend treat got markets off on solid footing as the week got underway.

Or Was It All Just a Dream?

An actual surprise (as opposed to the government backing away from previously announced tariffs, which at this point should surprise absolutely nobody) came on Thursday morning with the announcement that the US Court of International Trade (did you know that this was a thing prior to Thursday?) had issued a ruling that the government could not use the Emergency Economic Powers Act to unilaterally implement tariffs without Congressional approval.

As in – the base line 10 percent tariffs on everyone that were the remnant of the April 2 “Liberation Day” rates, along with the 25 percent hit to otherwise non-compliant goods from Mexico and Canada, and additionally the incremental tariffs on China – were all beyond the reach of the White House’s authority and were to be scrapped.

Investors around the world resembled nothing so much as Dorothy, waking up in her bed on the Kansas prairie, staring at Auntie Em and asking, was this all just a dream?

Not so fast, of course, as nothing in Year 2025 can ever simply just be. By the end of the day on Thursday, an appeals court had issued a stay on the USCIT ruling, meaning that the administration could continue collecting tariffs for now while the US Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit reviews the lower court’s decision.

Which could mean… anything from a green light to an outright ban to a “this is okay, but that is not okay” parsing of the potpourri of tariffs. For businesses trying to plan their strategies, for the poor beleaguered officials whose job it is to actually collect and document tariffs (yes, there are people who do this for a living, and they all deserve some kind of public service medal for their efforts to stay sane when this is all said and done), the uncertainty continues.

899, Not So Fine

And then there was this nugget, buried in the thousand-page tax bill trying to make its way through Congress, that intrepid sleuths dug out for public consideration just this morning: a tax surcharge on foreign investors deemed to be citizens of a country with “unfair tax policies” – a designation encompassing everywhere from most of the EU to Australia, the UK, Canada and elsewhere.

Investors from these countries, according to Section 899 of the “big, beautiful bill,” would pay additional taxes of 5 percent per year, up to a maximum of 20 percent, on dividends and interest from US stock and bond holdings (potentially including US Treasuries though the language on this is not clear in the bill).

In other words, for example, a foreign-owned company that wants to avoid punitive tariffs by investing in production facilities in the US could now be punished for that investment in the form of additional taxes on earnings flowing back to the home country.

Which seems like a very strange way to reward companies doing what the administration appeared to want them to do in the first place – export less and build more here. This also comes at a time when, as we discussed at length in our commentary last week, the financial position of the US is more reliant than ever on foreign creditors buying our debt.

Particularly long-term debt, where demand around the world is slacking, and the bond vigilantes are riding high. Weaker demand for US debt will, all else being equal, raise interest rates, which will increase the amount the government spends on interest payments, which is already more than it spends on defense, Medicare and Medicaid.

Will Section 899 make it through all the sausage-making that will take place between now and the bill’s final form? Plenty of evidence continues to suggest that when the market speaks in a loud and unhappy voice, this administration backs off.

The market – and its media mouthpieces – are speaking. The Economist magazine observes that Section 899 “would render America all-but-uninvestable for many foreigners.” Uninvestable is normally a term we associate with rickety frontier markets, not the 80 years-plus epicenter of global finance. Our policymakers need to deep-six 899 before it sees the light of day.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GE Aerospace: Shares Can't Keep Soaring Higher
data.description:
Great companies often command high prices on the market. Quality deserves a premium after all. Having said that, there is a point at which even the best businesses can be considered too expensive. One firm that is getting perilously close to that is GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE). Long before General Electric split up into multiple companies, I recognized that there was significant value for the firms in question. My overall call on this business has proven to be successful. Unfortunately, the market is now valuing shares of one of these, GE Aerospace at a price that is getting almost impossible to justify. I'm not ready to downgrade this enterprise from the ‘hold’ I reaffirmed it at in February of this year, to a ‘sell’ despite the stock rising 11.6% at a time when the S&P 500 is down 5.2%. But if the stock rises much further, a downgrade is almost guaranteed.

Investors Should Be Cautious

Fundamentally speaking, GE Aerospace is doing really well. It is an industry leader in the aerospace industry that is growing like a weed. As an example, we need only look at the most recent data available, which would cover the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, the business generated revenue of $9.94 billion. That represents an increase of 10.9% compared to the $8.96 billion reported the previous year.

Which really remarkable about this is that growth is occurring in spite of certain metrics of the company worsening. Take, for instance, its Commercial Engines & Services segment, which produces commercial airplane engines and provides services and replacement parts associated with them. This includes the famous LEAP, GEnx, and GE9X engines that have come to dominate the market. During the first quarter of 2025, the total number of commercial engines the company sold hit 444. That was, unfortunately, down from the 489 reported the previous year. This drop was driven by a decline in the number of LEAP engines sold from 369 to 319. Management blamed these declines on supply chain constraints.

You would think that revenue would have fallen in light of this. But revenue for both equipment and services managed to rise. Based on the data provided, equipment revenue grew from $1.71 billion to $1.86 billion. It's unclear exactly how much different things contributed to this increase. But management did mention in their quarterly report that the company saw improved customer mix and changes in pricing that positively affected it. The real driver of growth, however, was on the service side of things. Revenue there skyrocketed 16.6% from $6.10 billion to $5.12 billion. This was thanks to increased internal shop visit volume and shop visit workscope that the business is providing. Keep in mind that, each year, there are more of its engines on the market than ever before. This creates long-term demand for various services. It also helps that commercial air travel continues to expand. During the first quarter of this year, commercial air travel departures rose 4% year over year. And even though there are some near term concerns because of the trade war situation going on, in the long run, this trend can be expected to continue.

The company also saw a small bit of growth associated with its Defense & Propulsion Technologies segment. Overall sales inched up from $2.31 billion to $2.32 billion. This was driven almost entirely by its Propulsion & Additive Technologies, which reported growth from $817 million to $828 million. Management attributed this to aircraft systems product growth, changes in customer mix, and improvements in productivity. It is worth noting that this segment did see an increase in the number of defense engines sold from 125 to 131. Management said that the demand for its services in this segment continues to grow because of international government defense departments’ focus on modernizing and scaling their existing forces while simultaneously maintaining their existing operations. Considering that the US looks ready to increase military spending once again, and that Europe is likely to begin taking greater agency over its own security, I suspect that this trend will continue for the foreseeable future.

With revenue rising, profitability for the business continues to grow also. Net income jumped from $1.54 billion to $1.98 billion. Adjusted net profits grew similarly from $1.03 billion to $1.60 billion. There are, of course, other profitability metrics to take into consideration as well. It is true that operating cash flow contracted from $1.63 billion to $1.54 billion. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $1.38 billion to $1.44 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the company soared from $1.66 billion to $2.48 billion.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year as a whole, management has not provided any estimates of revenue. The only thing they did say was that, on an adjusted basis, it should grow at a low double-digit rate. But they did say that adjusted earnings per share should be between $5.10 and $5.45. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net income of $5.63 billion, which would represent an increase over the $5.04 billion reported last year. If we assume that other profitability metrics will rise at the same rate, this would mean adjusted operating cash flow of $7.64 billion and EBITDA of $7.20 billion.

Even with this tremendous growth and strong profitability, shares of the company look very expensive on an absolute basis. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean. These are very high trading multiples if you ask me. Admittedly, in this industry, trading multiples have a tendency to be high anyways. In the table below, you can see how the company stacks up against five similar firms. On both a price to earnings basis and on an EV to EBITDA basis, three of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. And on a price to operating cash flow basis, four of the five ended up being cheaper. While I do think that relative valuation is important, I do hold absolute valuation in higher regard. And to me, these multiples are getting very difficult to justify.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
GE Aerospace 48.8 35.9 39.1
RTX Corporation (RTX) 38.8 22.0 16.9
Boeing (BA) 50.1 38.0 112.4
Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSY) (OTCPK:EADSF) 29.6 14.4 12.8
Safran SA (OTCPK:SAFRY) (OTCPK:SAFRF) 52.0 24.2 146.9
Lockheed Martin (LMT) 20.3 16.5 14.1

This doesn't mean that I am anything other than bullish about the company in the long run. Operationally speaking, I think that it will do just fine. For starters, backlog for the company continues to grow. It hit $172.60 billion in the first quarter of this year. That's up from the $171.64 billion reported at the end of 2024. And it’s up quite materially from the $152.70 billion the business had in the first quarter of 2024. Management also continues to make investments in growth initiatives. For instance, this year, the firm is investing $1 billion in US manufacturing and hiring 5,000 workers here.

The company is also having some really interesting wins on the defense side of things. During the first quarter, for instance, it landed a new contract with the US Air Force that could be worth up to $5 billion in order to support foreign military sales for its F110-GE-129 engines that power both the F-15 and F-16 military aircraft. It's also making progress elsewhere, such as completing its initial ground runs for the T901 on a Black Hawk helicopter. The firm is also making additional progress for its new XA102 adaptive cycle engine. When it comes to its commercial operations, during the first quarter, the company also announced major deals with three major customers. All combined, these deals will involve over 100 engines.

Near term, the biggest risk factor for the company seems to be tariffs. However, management is not terribly concerned. They have not provided an estimate regarding the full cost of tariffs before factoring in any mitigation strategies. But they did say that they are using tools at their disposal like duty drawbacks and other strategies in order to optimize their operations, such as expanding foreign trade zones. The net cost after these actions should be around $500 million this year. However, the company is looking at ways to control costs without slashing investments, and potentially increasing prices, in order to offset this.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I think that GE Aerospace is a fantastic company. But honestly, it's a firm that is getting very expensive. I wouldn't go so far as to downgrade it just yet. After all, this is a fantastic company that is a true cash cow. It's also one that continues to return capital to shareholders. This year alone, for instance, management intends to allocate around $8 billion toward dividends and buybacks. But at some point, if the stock price keeps rising, a downgrade will be necessary.

data.title: Svenska Handelsbanken: Credit Can Be A Differentiator
data.description:
For all the drama surrounding U.S. trade policy, European financials continue to enjoy an excellent 2025, with the sector now up well over 40% in U.S. dollar terms. While it has slightly lagged, this rising tide has nevertheless been a boon for Sweden's Svenska Handelsbanken (OTCPK:SVNLF) (OTCPK:SVNLY), with Handelsbanken's ADSs returning around 36% so far this year, including a ~14% return since my last update in March.

Looking back, my bull case for Handelsbanken contained a few moving parts. Above the provisioning line, while the bank was seeing headwinds from lower interest rates, fee income growth and a much-welcome efficiency drive was at least providing a degree of support to earnings. Below it, well, credit quality has always been a standout feature of Handelsbanken. Given normalizing asset quality is a question of "when" rather than "if" for European banks, I figure this one is going too far better than most. Finally, Handelsbanken is sitting on one of the healthiest capital cushions in the space, and that could provide additional support to its dividend as earnings normalize.

With the above in mind, Handelsbanken's first quarter was reassuring, with the bank delivering more progress on fee growth and efficiency alongside another net credit release. While credit has always been a differentiator here, it has arguably gained even greater salience since my last update, with this period obviously encompassing the sweeping U.S. tariffs announced on "Liberation Day". Given Handelsbanken should be one of the least trade-sensitive European names I cover, I remain bullish.

Efficiency And Credit Continue To Provide Support

Having surged alongside rising interest rates, Handelsbanken's income continues to normalize now that policy rates are falling. As a remainder, Handelsbanken's revenue skews over 75% to net interest income. This is one of the highest shares among the European banks I cover and makes Handelsbanken relatively sensitive to changes in rates.

Last quarter, Handelsbanken generated SEK 11.347 billion in NII, down just over 3% sequentially. While day count negatively impacted the comp, NII was also down 2% year-on-year. Although Handelsbanken can't fully offset this, I have been looking it for to mitigate this headwind in two ways. Firstly, to grow its non-interest revenue, thereby limiting the decline in NII on overall income. Although NII dominates its top line, Handelsbanken still has good non-income lines of business, including in asset management, where it has a leading share of mutual fund inflows in Sweden.

This continues to play out. Last quarter, fee income rose by 5% year-on-year to SEK 2.9 billion, with fee income in the savings business rising 7% to nearly SEK 2 billion.

I have also been looking for support below the revenue line, namely in costs. Per the chart below, this had been a weak spot for the bank, with Handelsbanken previously displaying one of the worst cost-to-income trends among large Nordic peers.

Progress continues on this front. Last quarter, Handelsbanken reported operating costs of SEK 6.025 billion, down 7% on the year-ago period. Staff numbers declined by the same amount. While there would usually be a risk that personnel cuts could impact revenue, these cuts are not taking place at the branch-level, which remains key to Handelsbanken's business model.

As a result of these trends, pre-provision income has held up relatively well, falling by less than 1% year-on-year last quarter to SEK 8.763 billion.

Credit quality also continues to support earnings. Indeed, Handelsbanken actually recorded a modest SEK 54 million release last quarter (~1bp of total loans), its fifth straight period of positive provisioning. Credit has historically been one of the strongest aspects of this bank. Per the chart below, Handelsbanken simply doesn't lose much on the loans that it makes, even during extreme stress periods like the 2007-2009 financial crisis.

As you can see from the image below, residential and commercial mortgages account for the vast majority of Handelsbanken's loan book. Other forms of consumer and business lending are relatively limited.

Credit quality remains sound, with only 0.33% of Handelsbanken's loans non-performing last quarter. Of course, defaults are just one aspect of credit losses, as Handelsbanken can also recover sour debt through collateral. This also looks solid. In Sweden, Handelsbanken's weighted average loan-to-value ratio is around 60% in retail mortgages (and less on commercial mortgages). It is similarly conservative in its other markets like the Netherlands and the U.K., affording the bank a large collateral cushion should defaults rise.

Given U.S. tariffs, this feature of Handelsbanken's business has arguably taken on greater importance since my last update. While European banks with larger corporate exposure could see meaningful downside to their earnings in a bad case outcome, this is less of a risk for Handelsbanken.

Furthermore, Handelsbanken's balance sheet remains in excellent shape, with its CET1 Capital Ratio clocking in at 18.4% last quarter. This is 350bps above its regulatory minimum and 150bps above the mid-point of management's target range, leaving the door open for additional capital returns to shareholders.

Handelsbanken remains reasonably valued. As I type, its shares trade for SEK 128.05 ($6.67 per ADS), equal to 1.38x book value per share as of Q1. Per below, Handelsbanken has historically averaged a ~13% return on equity. Assuming a similar performance ahead, that would put its shares at a P/E of around 10.6x based on through-the-cycle profitability.

While that makes Handelsbanken slightly more expensive than some other European banks I follow, Handelsbanken's strong balance sheet and lower credit risk to tariffs warrant a premium. Moreover, Handelsbanken continues to offer a very high payout ratio. It paid out SEK 15 per share in respect of fiscal 2024, mapping to a yield of 11.7% and a payout ratio of 108% of EPS. Its above-average yield potential provides additional support to the stock.

Conclusion

Against contracting NII, Handelsbanken continues to deliver growing fee income and cost control, supporting its earnings. Credit quality has long been one of the strongest aspects of this bank. Given concerns that European economies may suffer as a result of U.S. tariffs, this feature of Handelsbanken's business may take on greater significance in the quarters ahead. Alongside its strong balance sheet and reasonable valuation, this keeps me bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
